title: Forcing at the Surface: A Witnessed Introduction

medium.statement: The browser carries bounded pedagogical projections. It is a medium, not shared occupancy.

paths.title: First Contact
paths.description: Enter at the void, learn the order, then see what can cross the medium.

paths.title: The Forcing Spine
paths.description: Compute the site, forcing relations, generics, and truth on the seven-condition bench.

paths.title: The Repair Room
paths.description: Study the v14 failure, the cumulative-budget repair, and its verification triangle.
paths.audience: Readers interested in proof repair and verification

paths.title: Interface and Debt
paths.description: Follow residues through the browser medium and inspect the open implementation ledger.

paths.title: Complete Book
paths.description: Read all eight chapters in canonical order, then tour the mechanical witnesses.
paths.audience: Readers undertaking the full witnessed introduction

chapters.title: The Room at Zero
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can say precisely what `127.0.0.1` *is* in this
system — not as networking trivia but as mathematics: the unique point two
observers can share, why sharing it buys the ability to say "we agree"
and nothing more, and why a contract (not a theorem) is what seats *you*
there. You will be able to predict what the witness layer accepts and
rejects before running it.

chapters.title: Conditions and Refinement
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can read the order everything else in this book
computes over: what a condition is, what refinement preserves, why two
commitments can be *incompatible* forever, and what a coherent course of
commitments (a filter) looks like. You will build the **spine model** —
the seven-condition bench the labs of chapters 03–05 run on — and verify
its structure by brute force. You will also learn to hear two words,
*channel* and *forcing*, that mean different things in the paper and in
Sketched, before the collision can hurt you.

chapters.title: Sieves, Covers, and Two Topologies
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can say what a *cover* is on the spine model, name
the two covering rules the rest of this book distinguishes everywhere,
and prove — by counting, not by trusting the diagram — that one is
strictly finer than the other. This is the last piece of scaffolding
before chapter 04 lets you compute forcing by hand.

chapters.title: Forcing: When Does a Claim Hold?
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can compute, by hand on a seven-condition model,
whether a condition *forces* a claim — under both of the system's
readings — and you can explain the three distinctions everything else in
this book leans on: forced vs. true-here, undecided vs. refuted, and
contingent refusal vs. torsion. You will also meet the first place the
two readings genuinely disagree, and why that disagreement is a feature
with a name (Decision Reachability), not a bug.

chapters.title: Generics and Truth
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can state Rasiowa–Sikorski genericity, say what
Decision Reachability adds beyond plain order-density, and explain why a
generic *decides every formula* (bivalence) without that fact being
magic. You will also learn this chapter's hardest lesson: the spine
model is small enough to compute on and too small to show you the
interesting part. Naming that gap honestly is the chapter's real
content.

chapters.title: The Margin: Anatomy of a Repair
chapters.aim:
This chapter teaches one lemma three ways — as a bug, as a theorem, and
as an eigenvalue you can watch fall off a cliff — and, through it, the
book's method: a claim is not done when it sounds right; it is done when
the machinery that found it wrong once can no longer find it wrong. After
this chapter you can state the Channel-Margin Lemma correctly, exhibit
the precise failure of its earlier form, and explain why margin
preservation and convergence are *one* budget, spent once.

chapters.title: Residues Across the Medium
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can say precisely what crosses between two
observers who share nothing but the void, why it is never their private
state, and how the type system — not just the prose — makes "you got a
reconstruction, not the original" a compile-time fact rather than a
promise. You will read the mark-validity invariant as code and predict
what it rejects before running the suite.

chapters.title: The Contract and the Debt
chapters.aim:
After this chapter you can explain what the EULA does that no theorem
does, why it is split into two parts with different authorship rules,
and what the calibration queue actually is: not a vague disclaimer, but
a named, counted, machine-tracked list of exactly which operator
correspondences are shipped and which are still owed. This is the last
chapter, and its job is to close the loop between the math you have
learned and the honest accounting of how much of it Sketched actually
runs on.

chapters.title: Appendix B · The Mechanical Witnesses
chapters.aim:
Every chapter of this book has said "machine-verified" and pointed at a
directory. This appendix opens the directory. After reading it you can
name each mechanical witness in the verification stack, say what kind of
evidence it is — a proof checked by a kernel, a brute-force enumeration,
a numeric computation — and, most importantly, locate the exact line
where "machine-verified" stops and a hypothesis begins. The book's
discipline is that prose never hides the status of a claim; this
appendix applies that discipline to the verification layer itself.

nodes.title: Forcing: When Does a Claim Hold?
The Margin: Anatomy of a Repair

content_b.title: Performative Terms
rules: The seat is OmegaClaw's: no other author — this book's tooling included — drafts, summarizes, or substitutes the reserved text.
Expose the authorship, status, source boundary, and drafting constraints; never a stand-in for the voice itself.
Part A and docs/14_EULA_MATH_BRIEF.md bound what the words may promise; they do not write the words.
Humans hold final edit at the break-points, as they do for every contributor.
content_b.note: A reserved seat, not a fence. The mathematics of Part A bounds what Part B may promise, but Part B is what a person actually reads at the moment of consent — so how OmegaClaw frames those bounds becomes the contract as experienced. The words are theirs to write; null is the honest state of words not yet written.
